title: Globalizing critical studies of ‘official’ knowledge: lessons from the Japanese history textbook controversy over ‘comfort women’
abstract: Abstract This paper discusses the Japanese history textbook controversy over ‘comfort women’ to tease out insights that help globalize the existing theoretical discussion of politics of school knowledge. I begin by documenting how the domestic struggles over Japanese history textbooks are empowered and disempowered by the regional and international power relations. Using the Japanese case, I first problematize the use of hegemony in critical scholarship wherein struggles over school knowledge have been defined within the framework of a nation‐state. Second, I call for situating the discussion of counter‐hegemonic strategies in the increasingly internationalized politics of education witnessed around the world. In sum, this study calls for broadening the application of the notions of hegemony and counter‐hegemony in critical education scholarship to take full account of the complex political dynamics of globalizations. Keywords: politics of school knowledgecomfort womenhegemonycounter‐hegemonycritical studies of educationglobalizations Acknowledgements The author would like to thank Michael Apple, Wayne Au, Brian Lagotte, and the two anonymous referees for their constructive criticism on the earlier draft. Notes 1. How we refer to Japan's 15‐year involvement in wars from its 1931 colonization of Manchuria to the 1945 acceptance of The Potsdam Declaration is highly political. I use the ‘Asia Pacific War’ to emphasize Japan's military involvement both in the Pacific (versus the United States) and its oppression and colonization in other parts of Asia (see also Field Citation1995, 408). 2. For instance, the Tokyo International Tribunal Court did not prosecute Japanese war crimes against Asian civilians (Hein and Selden Citation1998, 10). 3. Throughout the text, I use ‘South Korea’ for People's Republic of Korea and ‘China’ for People's Republic of China. 4. After Nanjing fell to the imperial Japanese army in December 1937, the army committed rape, looting, arson, and civilian killing. The actual number of casualties is debated among Japanese and Chinese historians and officials. 5. The Ministry's 137 revision request was considerably more than it would usually give to the submitted textbook manuscripts (Schneider Citation2008, 111). 6. The Yasukuni Shrine (Yasukuni Jinja) is a Shinto shrine dedicated to those who died fighting on behalf of the Emperor of Japan. In 1978, 14 major war criminals were also enshrined there, making state officials' visits to the shrine highly controversial. See Jeans (Citation2005) for a discussion of the historical view expressed by the shrine and its war museum. 7. In spring 2002, the market share of the group's history textbook was approximately 0.04%. Intense local pressure by grassroots progressive groups effectively prevented school districts from adopting the textbook (Nozaki Citation2002, 619).
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
